questions.question: Neither the students nor the teacher (ARE) happy with the new policy.
questions.explanation: When 'neither...nor' connects two subjects, the verb agrees with the subject closer to it. Here, 'teacher' is singular, so the verb should be 'is'.

questions.question: The manager, along with his team members, (WAS) praised for their excellent performance.
questions.explanation: When a singular subject is followed by phrases like 'along with', 'as well as', 'in addition to', the verb remains singular. 'The manager' is singular, so 'was' is correct.

questions.question: Having finished the report, (THEY SUBMITTED IT TO THE SUPERVISOR).
options: it was submitted to the supervisor
the report was submitted to the supervisor
questions.explanation: This sentence correctly uses a dangling participle. The subject 'they' is the one who finished the report and submitted it. No improvement is needed.

questions.question: She is one of the few scientists who (HAS) consistently challenged conventional theories.
questions.explanation: In relative clauses introduced by 'one of the few who/that', the verb agrees with the plural antecedent 'scientists', not 'one'. Therefore, 'have' is correct.

questions.question: The committee (ARE) divided in their opinion on the proposed changes.
questions.explanation: When a collective noun (like 'committee') refers to the group acting as a single unit, it takes a singular verb. If it refers to the individual members acting separately, it takes a plural verb. Here, 'divided in their opinion' implies individual opinions, so 'are' is correct. However, in formal English, especially for competitive exams, 'committee is divided' is often preferred when the division is the focus, treating the committee as a single entity with a divided opinion. But if the emphasis is on the individual members, 'are' is correct. Given the options, 'is' is the more commonly accepted answer for 'committee divided' in SSC CGL context, implying the committee as a single entity having a divided opinion.

questions.question: He not only writes novels but also (COMPOSE) poetry.
questions.explanation: This sentence requires parallel structure. Since 'writes' is in the simple present tense, 'compose' should also be in the simple present tense and agree with 'he', thus 'composes'.

questions.question: The old man, (WHOSE) hair was white, sat by the window.
questions.explanation: 'Whose' is the possessive pronoun, correctly indicating that the hair belongs to the old man. 'Who's' means 'who is' or 'who has'. 'Whom' is an object pronoun. 'Which' is for things. No improvement is needed.

questions.question: If I (WAS) a bird, I would fly to the highest mountain.
questions.explanation: In hypothetical or counterfactual conditional sentences (Type 2 conditional), 'if I were' is used for all persons, even for singular subjects, to express an unreal or improbable situation.

questions.question: The committee, along with its chairperson, (ARE) responsible for the final decision.
questions.explanation: The subject of the sentence is 'The committee' (singular), and 'along with its chairperson' is a prepositional phrase that does not affect the subject-verb agreement. Therefore, the singular verb 'is' should be used.

questions.question: Neither the students nor their teacher (WERE) able to solve the complex problem.
questions.explanation: When 'neither...nor' is used, the verb agrees with the noun or pronoun closest to it. In this case, 'teacher' is singular, so the verb should be 'was'.

questions.question: He is one of the few people who (ALWAYS SPEAK) the truth.
questions.explanation: The pronoun 'who' refers to 'people' (plural), so the verb associated with 'who' should also be plural ('speak'). The original sentence is correct.

questions.question: Having finished the assignment, the book was put away by the student.
options: Having finished the assignment, the student put away the book.
The assignment having been finished, the book was put away by the student.
The book was put away by the student after finishing the assignment.
questions.explanation: This is a dangling modifier. 'Having finished the assignment' should logically modify 'the student', not 'the book'. The corrected sentence clarifies who performed the action.

questions.question: The manager asked (HIM AND I) to prepare a detailed report by Friday.
questions.explanation: The pronouns are objects of the verb 'asked'. Therefore, objective case pronouns ('him' and 'me') should be used.

questions.question: She not only sings beautifully but also (PLAYING) the piano skillfully.
questions.explanation: This sentence requires parallel structure. Since 'sings' is a verb in the present tense, the second verb should also be in the present tense ('plays') to maintain parallelism with 'not only...but also'.

questions.question: If I (WAS) a bird, I would fly to you.
questions.explanation: This is a hypothetical or counterfactual conditional sentence (Type 2). In such sentences, 'were' is used for all subjects in the 'if' clause, regardless of singularity or plurality.

questions.question: The new policy will come (INTO) effect from next month.
questions.explanation: The phrasal verb 'come into effect' is the correct idiom meaning to become operative or valid. The original sentence is correct.

questions.question: Neither the students nor the teacher (WERE) present at the meeting.
questions.explanation: When 'neither...nor' connects two subjects, the verb agrees with the subject closer to it. In this case, 'the teacher' is singular, so the singular verb 'was' should be used.

questions.question: Having finished the assignment, the book was put back on the shelf by John.
options: Having finished the assignment, John put the book back on the shelf.
The assignment having been finished, John put the book back on the shelf.
After finishing the assignment, the book was put back on the shelf by John.
questions.explanation: This is a dangling modifier. 'Having finished the assignment' should logically modify 'John', not 'the book'. The corrected sentence makes it clear who finished the assignment.

questions.question: She is not only talented but also (HAS) a strong work ethic.
questions.explanation: For parallel structure with 'not only...but also', the elements following each part should be grammatically similar. 'Talented' is an adjective, so the second part should also be an adjective phrase or a verb phrase that matches the structure. 'Possesses a strong work ethic' maintains parallelism with 'talented' by using a verb.

questions.question: The manager asked (HE AND I) to submit the report by Friday.
questions.explanation: The pronouns are objects of the verb 'asked'. Therefore, objective case pronouns ('him' and 'me') should be used.

questions.question: The company has been (LOOKING UP) new ways to improve customer service.
questions.explanation: 'Looking into' means to investigate or examine, which fits the context of finding new ways to improve. 'Looking up' means to search for information, 'looking for' means to search to find something, and 'looking over' means to examine briefly.

questions.question: He (HAD BEEN WORKING) for three hours when he finally took a break.
questions.explanation: The past perfect continuous tense 'had been working' is correctly used here to indicate an action that started in the past and continued up to another point in the past (when he took a break).

questions.question: The committee, along with its chairperson, (ARE DISCUSSING) the new policy proposals.
questions.explanation: The subject of the sentence is 'The committee' (singular), and 'along with its chairperson' is a parenthetical phrase that does not affect the verb agreement. Therefore, the singular verb 'is discussing' is required.

questions.question: Neither the students nor their professor (WAS ABLE) to solve the complex equation.
questions.explanation: When 'neither...nor' connects two subjects, the verb agrees with the subject closer to it. In this case, 'professor' is singular, so 'was able' is correct. No improvement is needed.

questions.question: Having finished the report, (THE MANAGER FILED IT) neatly in the cabinet.
options: A. IT WAS FILED BY THE MANAGER
B. THE REPORT WAS FILED BY THE MANAGER
C. THE CABINET CONTAINED THE FILED REPORT
questions.explanation: This sentence correctly uses a dangling participle. The phrase 'Having finished the report' modifies 'the manager', who is the one performing the action. No improvement is needed.

questions.question: She not only excels in academics (BUT ALSO IN SPORTS).
options: A. BUT ALSO EXCELS IN SPORTS
B. BUT ALSO IN SPORTS SHE EXCELS
questions.explanation: For parallel structure with 'not only...but also', the grammatical structure following each part should be similar. Since 'excels in academics' is a verb phrase, 'excels in sports' is the correct parallel structure.

questions.question: The old man, (WHOSE EYESIGHT WAS FAILING), struggled to read the fine print.
questions.explanation: The relative pronoun 'whose' correctly indicates possession and introduces a descriptive clause about the old man's eyesight. The sentence is grammatically correct as it stands.

questions.question: If I (WAS) a bird, I would fly to the highest mountain peak.
questions.explanation: This is a hypothetical or counterfactual conditional sentence. In such cases, the subjunctive mood is used, requiring 'were' instead of 'was' for all subjects in the 'if' clause.

questions.question: The company has been (LOOKING FORWARD TO) expanding its operations overseas.
questions.explanation: The phrasal verb 'look forward to' means to anticipate with pleasure, and it is correctly followed by a gerund ('expanding'). No improvement is needed.

questions.question: He was so engrossed in his book that he (DID NOT HEAR) the doorbell ring.
questions.explanation: The simple past tense 'did not hear' is appropriate here to describe a single action that occurred in the past, concurrent with his engrossment. The sentence is grammatically correct.

questions.question: The committee members, along with the chairman, [was present] at the annual general meeting.
questions.explanation: The subject of the sentence is 'The committee members', which is plural. Phrases like 'along with', 'as well as', 'in addition to' do not affect the number of the subject. Therefore, the verb should be plural ('were present').

questions.question: Neither the students nor the teacher [were happy] with the new curriculum.
questions.explanation: When 'neither...nor' connects two subjects, the verb agrees with the subject closer to it. In this case, 'teacher' is singular, so the verb should be singular ('was happy').

questions.question: Having finished the report, [the manager submitted it to the director].
options: it was submitted by the manager to the director
the report was submitted to the director by the manager
the director received the report from the manager
questions.explanation: This sentence correctly uses a dangling participle. The subject of the main clause ('the manager') is the one who 'finished the report'. Therefore, 'No improvement' is required.

questions.question: She is not only talented but also [possesses a great deal of humility].
options: possessing a great deal of humility
having a great deal of humility
questions.explanation: The sentence demonstrates correct parallel structure. 'Is talented' and 'possesses a great deal of humility' are both verb phrases, maintaining grammatical balance. 'No improvement' is required.

questions.question: The old man, [who was walking slowly], stumbled and fell.
questions.explanation: The phrase 'who was walking slowly' is a correctly formed relative clause providing additional information about the old man. It is grammatically sound and does not require improvement.

questions.question: Choose the option that corrects the error in the sentence: She go to the market every day.
options: She goes to the market every day.
She went to the market every day.
She going to the market every day.
She gone to the market every day.
questions.explanation: For third-person singular subjects (she, he, it), the verb in simple present tense takes an '-es' ending.

questions.question: Identify the grammatically correct sentence:
options: The cat sat on the mat.
The cat sit on the mat.
The cat sitting on the mat.
The cat have sat on the mat.
questions.explanation: The simple past tense of 'sit' is 'sat'. This sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement and tense.

questions.question: The manager, along with his team members, are attending the conference next week.
options: The manager, along with his team members, is attending the conference next week.
The manager along with his team members are attending the conference next week.
The manager, along with his team members, attending the conference next week.
The manager and his team members, is attending the conference next week.
questions.explanation: When 'along with' connects subjects, the verb agrees with the first subject. 'Manager' is singular, so 'is attending' is correct.

questions.question: Having finished the report, the coffee break was a welcome relief.
options: Having finished the report, we welcomed the coffee break.
The report having been finished, the coffee break was a welcome relief.
Having finished the report, the coffee break was welcomed by us.
After finishing the report, the coffee break was a welcome relief.
questions.explanation: The original sentence suffers from a dangling participle; the subject of 'having finished' is unclear. The corrected option clarifies who finished the report.

questions.question: Neither of the two candidates have met the eligibility criteria for the position.
options: Neither of the two candidates has met the eligibility criteria for the position.
Neither of the two candidates have met the eligibility criteria for the position.
Neither of the two candidates meeting the eligibility criteria for the position.
Neither of the two candidates had met the eligibility criteria for the position.
questions.explanation: 'Neither' is singular and requires a singular verb. Therefore, 'has met' is the correct form.

questions.question: She is one of the brightest students who has ever graduated from this university.
options: She is one of the brightest students who have ever graduated from this university.
She is one of the brightest student who has ever graduated from this university.
She is one of the brightest students who ever graduated from this university.
She is one of the brightest students that has ever graduated from this university.
questions.explanation: In 'one of the X who/that Y', the verb Y refers to X (students), which is plural. Thus, 'have graduated' is correct.

questions.question: The committee is debating on the best course of action to take regarding the new policy.
options: The committee is debating the best course of action to take regarding the new policy.
The committee are debating on the best course of action to take regarding the new policy.
The committee is debating about the best course of action to take regarding the new policy.
The committee is debating for the best course of action to take regarding the new policy.
questions.explanation: The verb 'debate' is transitive and does not require a preposition like 'on' or 'about' when followed by a direct object. 'The best course of action' is the direct object.

questions.question:
Choose the sentence that is grammatically correct and stylistically superior:

(A) Hardly had the principal entered the auditorium then the students rose noisily from their seats, disrupting the speaker.
(B) No sooner had the principal entered the auditorium than the students rose noisily from their seats, disrupting the speaker.
(C) The principal had hardly entered the auditorium when the students rose noisily from their seats, disrupting the speaker.
(D) Barely had the principal entered the auditorium when the students rose noisily from their seats, disrupting the speaker.
questions.explanation: 'No sooner...than' is the correct correlative conjunction pair. Options A, C, and D use incorrect conjunctions or word order with the inverted structure.

questions.question:
Identify the sentence that best conveys the intended meaning without redundancy or grammatical errors:

(A) Despite of his persistent efforts, he failed to achieve his desired outcome, causing much disappointment.
(B) Even though he persistently tried, his desired outcome was not achieved, leading to much disappointment.
(C) Although he made persistent efforts, he failed to achieve his desired outcome, which caused much disappointment.
(D) His persistent efforts notwithstanding, he failed to achieve his desired outcome, consequently causing much disappointment.
questions.explanation: Option C correctly uses 'although' and avoids the redundant 'despite of'. Options A, B, and D have grammatical issues or awkward phrasing.

questions.question:
Select the most appropriate and grammatically sound sentence:

(A) Neither the manager nor his assistants was aware of the impending crisis, which could have been averted.
(B) Neither the manager nor his assistants were aware of the impending crisis, which could have been averted.
(C) Neither the manager nor his assistants were aware of the impending crisis; which could have been averted.
(D) Neither the manager nor his assistants was aware of the impending crisis; that could have been averted.
questions.explanation: When 'neither...nor' connects two subjects, the verb agrees with the subject closer to it. 'Assistants' is plural, so 'were' is correct, and 'which' correctly introduces a non-restrictive clause.

questions.question: Choose the part of the sentence that has an error: The dog wagged its tail happy when it saw its owner.
questions.explanation: 'Happy' is an adjective, but we need an adverb to describe how the dog wagged its tail. The correct word is 'happily'.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to complete the sentence: She ______ to the market every day.
questions.explanation: For a third-person singular subject like 'She' and a daily action, the simple present tense requires 'goes'.

questions.question: Which of the following sentences is grammatically correct?
options: Neither of the proposals were accepted by the committee.
Neither of the proposals was accepted by the committee.
Neither of the proposals are accepted by the committee.
Neither of the proposals have been accepted by the committee.
questions.explanation: 'Neither of' takes a singular verb. Therefore, 'was' is the correct verb form.

questions.question: Identify the sentence with the correct use of parallelism.
options: She enjoys hiking, swimming, and to cycle in the mountains.
She enjoys hiking, swimming, and cycling in the mountains.
She enjoys to hike, to swim, and cycling in the mountains.
She enjoys hiking, to swim, and cycling in the mountains.
questions.explanation: Parallelism requires consistent grammatical forms. All items in the list should be gerunds.

questions.question: Choose the best way to improve the underlined part of the sentence: 'Despite of his efforts, he could not win the race.'
questions.explanation: 'Despite' does not take 'of'. 'In spite of' or just 'Despite' are correct.

questions.question: Which sentence demonstrates proper subject-verb agreement?
options: The team of scientists are conducting an important experiment.
The team of scientists is conducting an important experiment.
A team of scientists were conducting an important experiment.
The team of scientists have conducted an important experiment.
questions.explanation: The subject is 'team' (singular), not 'scientists'. Therefore, the singular verb 'is' is required.

questions.question: Select the sentence that avoids a dangling modifier.
options: Running quickly, the bus was missed by John.
Running quickly, John missed the bus.
The bus was missed by John, running quickly.
Missed by John, the bus was running quickly.
questions.explanation: The introductory phrase 'Running quickly' must logically modify the subject 'John', not 'the bus'.

questions.question: Despite the unprecedented economic downturn, the company's profits, which had been declining for several quarters, surprisingly increased significantly, largely attributed to its aggressive new marketing strategy and a sudden surge in consumer demand.
options: Despite the unprecedented economic downturn, the company's profits, which had been declining for several quarters, surprisingly increased significantly, largely attributed to its aggressive new marketing strategy and a sudden surge in consumer demand.

Despite the unprecedented economic downturn, the company's profits, which had been declining for several quarters, surprisingly increased significantly; this was largely attributed to its aggressive new marketing strategy and a sudden surge in consumer demand.

Despite the unprecedented economic downturn, the company's profits, which had been declining for several quarters, surprisingly increased significantly, a fact largely attributed to its aggressive new marketing strategy and a sudden surge in consumer demand.

Despite the unprecedented economic downturn, the company's profits, which had been declining for several quarters, surprisingly increased significantly, largely due to its aggressive new marketing strategy and a sudden surge in consumer demand.
questions.explanation: The original sentence suffers from an unclear antecedent for 'attributed to'. Option C correctly uses 'a fact' to refer to the entire preceding clause, clarifying the attribution.

questions.question: The newly discovered ancient manuscript, deciphered by a team of international linguists, not only provides invaluable insights into a previously unknown civilization but also challenges long-held theories about the spread of early writing systems.
options: The newly discovered ancient manuscript, deciphered by a team of international linguists, not only provides invaluable insights into a previously unknown civilization but also challenges long-held theories about the spread of early writing systems.

The newly discovered ancient manuscript, deciphered by a team of international linguists, not only provides invaluable insights into a previously unknown civilization, but it also challenges long-held theories about the spread of early writing systems.

Deciphered by a team of international linguists, the newly discovered ancient manuscript not only provides invaluable insights into a previously unknown civilization but also challenges long-held theories about the spread of early writing systems.

The newly discovered ancient manuscript, deciphered by a team of international linguists, not only provides invaluable insights into a previously unknown civilization, but also challenges long-held theories about the spread of early writing systems.
questions.explanation: The original sentence correctly maintains parallel structure with 'not only provides... but also challenges'. Options B and D introduce unnecessary pronouns or incorrect punctuation.

questions.question: Having successfully completed the arduous expedition to the summit, the mountaineers were exhausted, but their spirits remained high, knowing they had achieved a feat few others had accomplished.
options: Having successfully completed the arduous expedition to the summit, the mountaineers were exhausted, but their spirits remained high, knowing they had achieved a feat few others had accomplished.

Having successfully completed the arduous expedition to the summit, the mountaineers were exhausted; however, their spirits remained high, knowing they had achieved a feat few others had accomplished.
Although exhausted from having successfully completed the arduous expedition to the summit, the mountaineers' spirits remained high, knowing they had achieved a feat few others had accomplished.
Successfully completing the arduous expedition to the summit, the mountaineers were exhausted, but their spirits remained high, knowing they had achieved a feat few others had accomplished.
questions.explanation: The original sentence has a slight dangling modifier issue with 'knowing' implying the mountaineers were actively knowing while exhausted. Option C rephrases for better flow and clarity, making the exhaustion conditional to the high spirits.

questions.question: Identify the part of the sentence that contains an error: "She sings very good, and everyone enjoys her performance."
questions.explanation: 'Good' is an adjective, but an adverb is needed to modify the verb 'sings'. The correct word should be 'well'.

questions.question: Choose the correct sentence: "Among the two sisters, Mary is the tallest."
options: Among the two sisters, Mary is the tallest.
Between the two sisters, Mary is the taller.
Between the two sisters, Mary is the tallest.
Among the two sisters, Mary is taller.
questions.explanation: Use 'between' for two items and the comparative degree ('taller') when comparing two things.

questions.question: Select the option that corrects the error in the underlined part of the sentence: The committee decided to postpone its meeting, *due to the fact that many members were absent*.
options: on account of many members being absent.
due to many members were absent.
owing to the fact that many members were absent.
questions.explanation: 'Due to the fact that' is wordy and can be replaced with 'because'. 'Due to' typically modifies nouns, not verbs or clauses.

questions.question: Choose the best option to improve the underlined part of the sentence: She is one of the most brilliant students *who has ever attended this university*.
options: who have ever attended this university.
that has ever attended this university.
which has ever attended this university.
questions.explanation: The relative pronoun 'who' refers to 'students' (plural), so the verb should be plural 'have'. This ensures subject-verb agreement with the antecedent.

questions.question: Identify the sentence that is grammatically correct and conveys the intended meaning: Despite of the heavy rain, the match continued.
options: Despite the heavy rain, the match continued.
In spite of the heavy rain, the match continued.
Despite of heavy rain, the match continued.
Even though the heavy rain, the match continued.
questions.explanation: The correct idiom is 'despite' (without 'of') or 'in spite of'. 'Despite of' is grammatically incorrect.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to replace the underlined part: The manager, along with his team, *are responsible for the project's success*.
options: is responsible for the project's success.
were responsible for the project's success.
are being responsible for the project's success.
have been responsible for the project's success.
questions.explanation: When 'along with' connects two subjects, the verb agrees with the first subject ('The manager'). Therefore, the singular verb 'is' is required.

questions.question: Which of the following sentences is free from grammatical errors?
options: Neither the students nor the teacher were present.
Either John or Mary are going to attend the conference.
Not only the parents but also the child was surprised.
Every one of the books on the shelf are new.
questions.explanation: With 'not only...but also', the verb agrees with the subject closer to it ('the child'). The other options have subject-verb agreement errors.

questions.question: The newly discovered ancient manuscript, which archaeologists believe to have been written by a priestess of a forgotten cult, contains a series of cryptic prophecies whose meaning has eluded scholars for centuries, despite their exhaustive efforts to decipher it.
options: The newly discovered ancient manuscript, which archaeologists believe was written by a priestess of a forgotten cult, contains a series of cryptic prophecies whose meaning has eluded scholars for centuries, despite their exhaustive efforts to decipher them.

The newly discovered ancient manuscript, which archaeologists believe to be written by a priestess of a forgotten cult, contains a series of cryptic prophecies whose meaning has eluded scholars for centuries, despite their exhaustive efforts to decipher it.

The newly discovered ancient manuscript, which archaeologists believe to have been written by a priestess of a forgotten cult, contains a series of cryptic prophecies whose meaning has eluded scholars for centuries, despite their exhaustive efforts to decipher them.
questions.explanation: The original sentence has an incorrect tense in 'to have been written' and a pronoun agreement error with 'it' referring to 'prophecies'. The corrected option addresses both issues, using 'was written' for a completed past action and 'them' to refer to 'prophecies'.

questions.question: Having successfully negotiated the complex geopolitical landscape and securing crucial alliances, the diplomat's tireless efforts were instrumental in averting a regional crisis, a feat that few believed possible given the prevailing tensions.
options: Having successfully negotiated the complex geopolitical landscape and secured crucial alliances, the diplomat's tireless efforts were instrumental in averting a regional crisis, a feat that few believed possible given the prevailing tensions.

Successfully negotiating the complex geopolitical landscape and securing crucial alliances, the diplomat's tireless efforts were instrumental in averting a regional crisis, a feat that few believed possible given the prevailing tensions.

Having successfully negotiated the complex geopolitical landscape and having secured crucial alliances, the diplomat's tireless efforts were instrumental in averting a regional crisis, a feat that few believed possible given the prevailing tensions.
questions.explanation: The original sentence suffers from a faulty parallelism between 'negotiated' and 'securing'. Both participles should be in the same form ('negotiated' and 'secured') to maintain grammatical consistency.

questions.question: Neither the unprecedented economic downturn nor the persistent public outcry against the proposed policy, which many economists deemed fiscally irresponsible, were enough to sway the government from its predetermined course of action.
options: Neither the unprecedented economic downturn nor the persistent public outcry against the proposed policy, which many economists deemed fiscally irresponsible, was enough to sway the government from its predetermined course of action.

Neither the unprecedented economic downturn nor the persistent public outcry against the proposed policy, which many economists deemed fiscally irresponsible, were enough to sway the government of its predetermined course of action.

Neither the unprecedented economic downturn nor the persistent public outcry against the proposed policy, which many economists deemed fiscally irresponsible, have been enough to sway the government from its predetermined course of action.
questions.explanation: According to the rule of subject-verb agreement with 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the noun closest to it ('public outcry'). Since 'public outcry' is singular, the verb should be 'was', not 'were'.

questions.question: The new policy have been implemented last year.
questions.explanation: The subject 'policy' is singular, so the verb 'has' is used. This is a basic concept of subject-verb agreement.

questions.question: By the time I arrived, they have eaten dinner.
questions.explanation: The correct sentence should be 'By the time I arrived, they had eaten dinner' to indicate an action completed before another action.

questions.question: The number of students in the class are increasing.
questions.explanation: The subject 'number' is singular, so the verb 'is' is used. This is a basic concept of subject-verb agreement.

questions.question: The teacher give us a lot of homework.
questions.explanation: The subject 'teacher' is singular, so the verb 'gives' is used. This is a basic concept of subject-verb agreement.

questions.question: The company will hire whoever are willing to work hard.
questions.explanation: The subject 'whoever' is singular, so the verb 'is' is used. This is a basic concept of subject-verb agreement.

questions.question: The new employee was very nervous on he first day.
questions.explanation: The correct sentence should be 'The new employee was very nervous on his first day' to indicate possession.

questions.question: The manager asked the employees to not come late.
questions.explanation: The correct sentence should be 'The manager asked the employees not to come late' to indicate a negative instruction.

questions.question: The teacher told the students that they was going to the museum.
questions.explanation: The correct sentence should be 'The teacher told the students that they were going to the museum' to indicate the correct verb form.

questions.question: The new policy has been implement last year.
questions.explanation: The correct sentence should be 'The new policy has been implemented last year' to indicate the correct verb form.

questions.question: The number of students in the class is less than what it were last year.
questions.explanation: The correct sentence should be 'The number of students in the class is less than what it was last year' to indicate the correct verb form.

questions.question: The new policy will be implemented by the government next year, it will bring about many changes.
questions.explanation: The sentence is a comma splice and needs a conjunction for correction, 'There will be' is the correct option to introduce the changes.

questions.question: Having studied the lessons, the exam was written by me.
questions.explanation: The sentence is in passive voice, it should be converted to active voice for better meaning.

questions.question: The teacher as well as the students were surprised.
questions.explanation: The subject 'The teacher' is singular, but 'as well as' phrase does not make it plural.

questions.question: The company will hire whoever is most qualified for the position.
questions.explanation: The subject of the sentence is 'whoever', the correct object form should be used after 'is'.

questions.question: If I was you, I would not do that.
questions.explanation: The correct form of 'to be' in the subjunctive mood should be used.

questions.question: Neither of the students have done the homework.
questions.explanation: The subject 'neither' is singular, the verb should agree with it.

questions.question: The team are going for a vacation.
questions.explanation: The subject 'team' is singular, the verb should agree with it.

questions.question: The new employee was very nervous on his first day, but he quickly adjusted.
questions.explanation: The sentence is in the past tense, 'was' is the correct form to use.

questions.question: The manager asked the employees why they hasn't attended the meeting.
questions.explanation: The sentence should use the present perfect tense 'haven't' for correct meaning.

questions.question: The new policy, which was announced last week, are expected to change the industry.
questions.explanation: The subject 'policy' is singular, the verb should agree with it.

questions.question: The new policy will effect many employees.
questions.explanation: The correct word to use here is 'affect' as it means to have an influence on something.

questions.question: The teacher gave the students they're homework back.
questions.explanation: The correct word to use here is 'their' as it shows possession.

questions.question: The new employee was very nervous on he's first day.
questions.explanation: The correct word to use here is 'his' as it shows possession.

questions.question: The tour guide explained the history of the castle to we tourists.
questions.explanation: The correct word to use here is 'us' as it is the object of the preposition.

questions.question: The teacher asked the students to write a essay about their summer vacation.
